figures.gloss: The farm boy of New York's revival-burned 'burned-over district' who reported a vision of the Father and Son (1820), received golden plates from the angel Moroni, and dictated the Book of Mormon largely through a seer stone. He organized the church on 6 April 1830, built Kirtland and Nauvoo, introduced the temple rites, and was killed by a mob at Carthage Jail on 27 June 1844.
figures.facet: Not a reformer but a restorer: where Luther pointed back to a text, Smith claimed the heavens had reopened — new scripture, new priesthood, new ordinances — making revelation itself, not interpretation, the movement's engine.
figures.provenance: Joseph Smith—History (Pearl of Great Price); Richard Bushman, Joseph Smith: Rough Stone Rolling (2005); Fawn Brodie, No Man Knows My History (1945).

figures.gloss: In the Book of Mormon's own account, the last Nephite prophet-historian, who sealed the record and buried the plates about 421 CE — and who returned as a resurrected, embodied messenger to the seventeen-year-old Smith in 1823, meeting him yearly at the hill Cumorah until the plates were delivered in 1827. His trumpet-blowing figure crowns most Latter-day Saint temples.
figures.facet: Scripture delivered by its own dead author, now embodied rather than disembodied: the ancient chronicler personally hands over his book fourteen centuries later as a resurrected being, not a ghost — the ancestor as archivist, collapsing the gap between composition and canon while the tradition insists on the physicality of the messenger.
figures.provenance: Joseph Smith—History 1:27–54; Book of Mormon (Mormon 8; Moroni 10).

figures.gloss: Published at Palmyra in March 1830: presented as the record of Israelite families led to the Americas around 600 BCE, culminating in the risen Christ's ministry to them — 'Another Testament of Jesus Christ,' and in Smith's words the 'keystone' of the religion. Its ancient historicity is rejected by mainstream archaeology and genetics and held within the church by faith.
figures.facet: The canon reopened: the claim is not a better reading of the Bible but a second Bible of equal standing — the boldest scriptural move in American religious history, and the seam along which the tradition parted from Protestantism.
figures.provenance: The Book of Mormon (1830); Terryl Givens, By the Hand of Mormon (2002).

figures.gloss: The temple's central ordinance, first administered by Smith to nine men in the upper room of Nauvoo's Red Brick Store on 4 May 1842: washing and anointing, a dramatized creation-and-fall narrative, and covenants with signs and tokens, open only to members holding a temple recommend. Vicarious endowment for the dead extends the rite across the generations.
figures.facet: A genuine graded mystery inside modern Christianity: the tradition maintains an interior, access-controlled ritual world that even its own members must qualify to enter — sacred secrecy institutionalized, not residual.
figures.provenance: David John Buerger, The Mysteries of Godliness (1994); Michael W. Homer, Joseph's Temples (2014).

figures.gloss: The doctrine that human spirits existed before birth — uncreated 'intelligences' organized as spirit children of God (Book of Abraham 3; D&C 93) — and that the faithful, sealed in temple marriage, may progress to godhood. Smith preached it fullest in the King Follett discourse (7 April 1844); Lorenzo Snow's couplet compressed it: 'As man now is, God once was; as God now is, man may be.'
figures.facet: It closes the ontological gap most theisms guard: God and humanity differ in degree, not kind — one eternal species at different points on the same road, with premortal existence as the road's beginning.
figures.provenance: King Follett Discourse (1844); Doctrine & Covenants 76, 93, 132; Pearl of Great Price, Book of Abraham 3.

figures.gloss: The principle that the church president is a living 'prophet, seer, and revelator' and the canon remains open: the Doctrine & Covenants collects revelations as scripture, and course-changing declarations — the 1890 Manifesto ending plural marriage, the 1978 revelation extending priesthood to Black members — carry canonical force.
figures.facet: Doctrine with a steering wheel: the same office that produced the canon can amend it, letting the tradition make binding reversals no fixed-canon church can — at the price of making history itself a theological instrument.
figures.provenance: Doctrine & Covenants (1835– ), Official Declarations 1 (1890) and 2 (1978); Ninth Article of Faith.

figures.gloss: Joseph Smith's successor as church president (1801–1877), who led the majority of the Saints on the 1846–47 exodus from Nauvoo to the Salt Lake Valley, governed Utah Territory as its first governor, directed the settlement of some 350 Great Basin communities, and publicly avowed plural marriage as church doctrine in 1852. His nearly thirty-year presidency fixed the institutional and cultural form the church carried through its Utah century.
figures.facet: The tradition's second founding: where Smith supplied the revelation, Young supplied the state — the Utah theocracy, the colonization grid, and the public defense of polygamy are his work, and without them the movement that survived 1844 is unexplainable.
figures.provenance: Leonard J. Arrington, Brigham Young: American Moses (1985); John G. Turner, Brigham Young: Pioneer Prophet (2012).

figures.gloss: A Mother in Heaven taught alongside the Father from the Nauvoo period, fixed in popular devotion by Eliza R. Snow's hymn 'O My Father' (1845, 'In the heav'ns are parents single? / No, the thought makes reason stare'), and affirmed institutionally in the church's 1995 'The Family: A Proclamation to the World' ('heavenly parents'). She is not prayed to directly, and church teaching on her nature and role remains deliberately undeveloped.
figures.facet: A female divinity held at doctrinal arm's length: the church affirms her existence and reticence toward her simultaneously — she is real enough to name and cite in official proclamations, yet withheld from prayer and left almost entirely undescribed, silence itself functioning as part of the doctrine.
figures.provenance: Eliza R. Snow, 'My Father in Heaven' / 'O My Father' (1845); 'The Family: A Proclamation to the World' (1995); David L. Paulsen and Martin Pulido, 'A Mother There: A Survey of Historical Teachings about Mother in Heaven,' BYU Studies (2011).

figures.gloss: The 'new and everlasting covenant' of polygamy (Doctrine & Covenants 132), practiced secretly by Joseph Smith and a small circle from the early-to-mid 1830s–40s and publicly avowed in Utah from 1852; it structured elite and ordinary Mormon family life for decades before the 1890 Manifesto ended new plural marriages under federal legal pressure (the Edmunds and Edmunds-Tucker Acts).
figures.facet: A revelation that reorganized kinship itself: plural marriage was defended not as a concession but as a higher law of exaltation, making the family structure of heaven — not merely its theology — the doctrine's real stakes, and its abandonment split the movement into the mainstream church and enduring fundamentalist offshoots.
figures.provenance: Doctrine & Covenants 132; Official Declaration 1 (the 1890 Manifesto); Kathryn M. Daynes, More Wives Than One: Transformation of the Mormon Marriage System, 1840–1910 (2001).

figures.gloss: Vicarious baptism performed by a living proxy in a temple font (traditionally borne on the backs of twelve oxen) on behalf of a named deceased person, taught by Joseph Smith from 1840 on the authority of 1 Corinthians 15:29; it is the ordinance that drives the church's large-scale genealogical research program, since a name and basic vital data are required before the rite can be performed.
figures.facet: Salvation run backward through the family tree: rather than closing at death, the ordinance system is extended retroactively to every traceable ancestor, making genealogical record-keeping itself a sacred labor and turning the whole human family, living and dead, into the rite's intended congregation.
figures.provenance: Doctrine & Covenants 124, 127–128; 1 Corinthians 15:29; the church's FamilySearch genealogical program is the institutional expression of this doctrine's data requirements.

figures.gloss: Joseph Smith's account of a vision, at about age fourteen, of the Father and the Son appearing together in a grove near Palmyra, New York, in answer to his prayer over which church to join; he reported being told to join none of them. Smith produced several accounts across his lifetime with differing emphases and details, the earliest surviving handwritten version dating to 1832, the now-canonical version to 1838.
figures.facet: The founding theophany carries its own textual problem inside it: the church's argument that the heavens reopened rests on an event whose earliest written record postdates it by twelve years and whose details shifted across later retellings — the movement's origin point is simultaneously its most examined historical seam.
figures.provenance: Joseph Smith—History 1:14–20 (Pearl of Great Price, 1838 account); the 1832 holograph account (Joseph Smith Letterbook 1); James B. Allen, 'The Significance of Joseph Smith's First Vision in Mormon Thought,' Dialogue (1966).

figures.gloss: A revelation received by Joseph Smith in 1833 (Doctrine & Covenants 89) counseling against wine and strong drink, tobacco, and 'hot drinks' (interpreted early on as coffee and tea), and commending grain and herbs; treated as optional counsel for decades, it was made a binding requirement for temple attendance ('temple recommend' worthiness) in the early 20th century under church president Heber J. Grant.
figures.facet: A dietary counsel that became the tradition's most visible daily boundary marker: the Word of Wisdom now does more to mark a practicing Latter-day Saint in ordinary social life — what is or isn't ordered at a restaurant table — than any belief statement does, doctrine legible in the body's small daily refusals.
figures.provenance: Doctrine & Covenants 89 (1833); Thomas G. Alexander, 'The Word of Wisdom: From Principle to Requirement,' Dialogue (1981), tracing its shift from optional counsel to binding requirement.

figures.gloss: The two orders of restored priestly authority underlying every ordinance in the tradition: the lesser, Aaronic priesthood, said to have been conferred on Smith and Oliver Cowdery by the resurrected John the Baptist in 1829, and the higher, Melchizedek priesthood, conferred (per church accounts) by the ancient apostles Peter, James and John. Historically withheld from Black members until a 1978 revelation (Official Declaration 2) extended it to all worthy male members regardless of race.
figures.facet: Authority claimed as a mechanism, not a metaphor: the tradition does not merely assert continuity with the ancient church but names the specific angelic ordainers and dates of transfer, and holds this authority in essentially every adult male member rather than a professional clergy — a lay priesthood of millions, extended and restricted by revelation across the tradition's own history.
figures.provenance: Joseph Smith—History 1:68–72; Doctrine & Covenants 13, 27, 84, 107; Official Declaration 2 (1978).

figures.gloss: The afterlife map given in Smith's 1832 vision recorded as Doctrine & Covenants 76: three graded kingdoms — celestial, terrestrial, and telestial — into which nearly all of humanity is ultimately assigned some measure of glory, with only 'sons of perdition' cast into outer darkness; exaltation into the highest degree of the celestial kingdom is reserved for those sealed in covenant temple marriage and faithful to its terms.
figures.facet: A near-universalist afterlife wearing a hierarchical face: almost no one is damned outright, yet the kingdoms are sharply graded and the very summit is closed to all but the covenant-married — the tradition inverts the Protestant heaven/hell binary into a spectrum, then re-imports exclusivity at the top of that spectrum rather than at its bottom.
figures.provenance: Doctrine & Covenants 76 (the 'Vision,' 1832); Doctrine & Covenants 131:1–4 on the celestial kingdom's three sub-degrees and the requirement of temple marriage for its highest.

edges.note: The book is Smith's founding act and credential — the 'keystone' whose existence is the movement's argument for his prophethood.
Moroni is at once the book's final ancient author and its modern deliverer — custodian of the record on both sides of the grave.
The endowment and temple sealing are the ordinance-track to exaltation — the rite exists to move candidates along the road the doctrine names.

GOLD SEAM: Restorationist descent — the movement arose inside Second Great Awakening Protestantism and claims to restore, not reform, Christ's primitive church; the same Christ, radically reframed by an embodied Father and a non-Nicene Godhead.

GOLD SEAM: Smith was raised a Master Mason in Nauvoo on 15–16 March 1842; the endowment, introduced seven weeks later, carried documented Masonic ritual elements — signs, tokens, penalties, the five points of fellowship (the penalties removed in 1990).

GOLD SEAM: Both teach human deification, but the kinship is structural, not genetic — theosis preserves the creature/Creator divide as participation in divine energies, while exaltation dissolves it into a single eternal species.
both institutionalize ongoing prophetic guidance after the founding revelator — an elected caliphate held to be divinely guided against a living-prophet office with an open canon.

two unrelated 19th-20th century restorationist movements each ground their founding prophet's authority in a claim that the original Christian church wholly apostatized — Joseph Smith's First Vision account and the INC's Total Apostasy doctrine — independently arriving at the same restorationist premise.

Two 19th/20th-century American movements each producing their own authoritative English-language scriptural apparatus to anchor a claim of restored or corrected truth against mainstream Christianity — though the NWT is a translation of the existing Bible and the Book of Mormon a newly revealed text, a real structural difference kept at low tier.
Young is Smith's chosen successor in practice, if not by uncontested formal designation — the martyred founder and the man who carried the movement west and gave it institutional permanence.
Young's 1847 succession as church president is the first working instance of the living-prophet office the continuing-revelation principle describes.
Young made plural marriage a public, defended institution of the Utah church in 1852, moving it from Smith's private practice into open doctrine and social order.

Two new religious movements whose founding prophet's death was followed by an enduring line of successor-leadership that carried and consolidated the community — Young's presidency and the Ahmadiyya Khilafat, structurally parallel successions, no historical connection.

Two 19th-century American new religious movements that posited a female divine parent alongside a male God — Heavenly Mother and Holy Mother Wisdom — a structural rhyme of the same restorationist milieu, not a demonstrated borrowing between the traditions.

Heavenly Mother is the doctrinal completion exaltation's premortal-spirit-children teaching implies: if the faithful become spirit parents in eternity, a Mother in Heaven is the pattern's origin as much as its destination.
The 1890 Manifesto ending new plural marriages is itself the continuing-revelation principle's most consequential exercise — the same doctrinal mechanism that instituted the practice also ended it.

Baptism for the dead and the vicarious endowment are the two proxy ordinances performed in temple fonts and rooms on behalf of the deceased — the same vicarious logic applied to the two founding rites of the tradition.

Both traditions hold that the living can act to improve a departed soul's standing after death — proxy temple baptism against intercessory prayer and Mass for souls in purgatory — though the mechanisms differ sharply (a repeatable ordinance versus accumulated merit and prayer).
The First Vision is Smith's founding credential — the theophany the whole prophetic career and the period note's 1820 anchor point rest on.
The First Vision opens the sequence of heavenly visitations Moroni continues three years later — the reopened heavens' first and second messengers.

Two American Restorationist traditions each codified a distinctive dietary discipline (avoid alcohol, tobacco, and for Adventists also meat) as a marker of bodily holiness, both emerging from the same 19th-century American health-reform milieu without one tradition borrowing directly from the other.
The restoration of priesthood authority to Smith by angelic ordination is the claimed mechanism underlying his authority to organize the church and administer every ordinance in the roster.
The 1978 revelation extending priesthood ordination to Black members (Official Declaration 2) is a direct exercise of the continuing-revelation principle upon the priesthood doctrine itself.
Exaltation is attainment of the highest sub-degree of the celestial kingdom — the doctrine of eternal progression names the goal the three-kingdom map locates.

Two 19th-century American-adjacent revelations replace a binary heaven/hell with graded afterlife states admitting nearly everyone to some degree of glory — Smith's three kingdoms and Swedenborg's heaven-and-hell-as-states, independently arrived at.

Two esoteric annexations of Hebrews 7's priest 'without genealogy': Urantia incarnates Melchizedek as a celestial teacher in Abraham's Salem, while LDS names its higher priesthood after him without claiming he is embodied in the tradition — shared source text, no descent between the two readings.

dossier.essence: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints is the restorationist church organized by Joseph Smith in upstate New York in 1830 on the claim that the heavens had reopened: God had called a new prophet, restored the ancient priesthood, and delivered new scripture in the Book of Mormon. Headquartered in Salt Lake City after the exodus of 1846-47, it counts roughly seventeen million members worldwide and continues to expand its global temple network.
dossier.cosmos: Latter-day Saint cosmology closes the gap between God and humanity: God the Father is an exalted, embodied being, human spirits are his premortal children organized from uncreated 'intelligences,' and mortal life is a probationary schooling within a 'plan of salvation.' At death spirits enter a spirit world to await a universal resurrection and assignment to graded kingdoms of glory — celestial, terrestrial, telestial — while the faithful, sealed by temple ordinances, may progress toward exaltation: godhood in eternal families, on the road Lorenzo Snow's couplet compressed to 'as God now is, man may be.'
dossier.practice: The church runs on an unpaid lay priesthood — no professional clergy — with weekly sacrament meetings, tithing, the Word of Wisdom's abstention from alcohol, tobacco, coffee, and tea, and a signature corps of young full-time missionaries. Temple worship stands apart from Sunday worship: the endowment, the sealing of marriages and families, and vicarious ordinances for the dead (the engine of the church's vast genealogical enterprise), open only to members holding a temple recommend. A living prophet-president and the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles govern by continuing revelation.
dossier.canon: An open canon of four 'standard works' — the Bible (King James Version), the Book of Mormon (1830), the Doctrine and Covenants, and the Pearl of Great Price — to which the living prophet's canonized declarations can still add.
dossier.reflects: The impulse to restore rather than reform — reopening the canon and the prophetic office itself, so that revelation operates as an ongoing institution rather than a closed inheritance.
sources: Richard Lyman Bushman, 'Joseph Smith: Rough Stone Rolling' (2005)
Terryl L. Givens, 'By the Hand of Mormon: The American Scripture that Launched a New World Religion' (2002)
Jan Shipps, 'Mormonism: The Story of a New Religious Tradition' (1985)
Joseph Smith Jr. (trans.), 'The Book of Mormon' (1830)
